title: U-Bahn vs S-Bahn - Quick Summary
items: **The U-Bahn is the underground metro; the S-Bahn is the mostly-overground city rail.** U-Bahn (blue **U**) is run by BVG and burrows densely through the centre. S-Bahn (green **S**) is run by Deutsche Bahn, mostly runs above ground on longer routes, and even reaches out to Potsdam.

**One ticket covers both, plus trams and buses.** This is the single most useful thing to know: a normal Berlin **AB ticket** works on the U-Bahn, S-Bahn, tram and bus without buying anything extra when you switch. You never need a separate ticket to change from a U to an S.

**Tell them apart by the letter and the colour.** On the map and on signs, U-Bahn is a **blue U**, S-Bahn is a **green S in a circle**. The trains look different too: U-Bahn cars are yellow, S-Bahn cars are the red-and-cream ones, often up on brick viaducts.

**Use the U-Bahn for short central hops, the S-Bahn for distance.** Between Alexanderplatz, Museum Island and Kreuzberg, the U-Bahn is faster. To cross the whole city, reach the airport, or ride the **Ringbahn** (S41 clockwise, S42 anticlockwise), the S-Bahn wins.

**Both run all night on Friday and Saturday.** Midweek they run roughly 4:00 to 1:00, with N-prefixed night buses filling the gap. On weekend nights you can ride the U-Bahn and S-Bahn around the clock.

**Do not overthink it.** Look at the letter, get on, tap nothing (there are no gates), but do validate a paper ticket before boarding. For most first-day sightseeing you will barely touch anything except the U-Bahn.
